---
bibliography: "../input/bib/merit-factorial.bib"
csl: "../input/bib/apa6.csl"
---

# Conclusion

Meritocratic beliefs remain central to how inequality is interpreted, and schools are key settings in which they are produced and reinforced through evaluation, tracking, and selection [@chauvin_school_2026; @batruch_belief_2022; @mijs_unfulfillable_2016; @resh_sense_2014; @traini_climbing_2025]. However, research on adolescents’ meritocratic beliefs has often relied on unidimensional measures that conflate perceptions with preferences and treat meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles as opposites. Extending Castillo et al.’s [-@castillo_multidimensional_2023] framework to adolescents, this study tests whether students distinguish between perceived and preferred meritocracy and non-meritocracy, whether merit endorsement can coexist with recognition of non-meritocratic advantage, and whether this model is stable across school stages and over time in Chile.

Our results support a four-factor structure—perceived meritocracy, perceived non-meritocracy, preferred meritocracy, and preferred non-meritocracy—showing that adolescents differentiate between perceptions and preferences, and between merit and non-merit, and that endorsement of merit can coexist with recognition of non-meritocratic advantage. These findings support modeling school meritocracy as distinct yet related dimensions rather than collapsing them into a single score, particularly when using zero-sum indices. The scale is strictly invariant over time for individual students, providing a robust basis for longitudinal analysis, but it does not achieve cross-level equivalence; therefore, comparisons across educational stages require caution. More broadly, what is often interpreted as a change in belief in meritocracy may partly reflect increasing recognition of non-meritocratic constraints rather than declining endorsement of merit, consistent with recent accounts of dual consciousness [@tang_meritocratic_2025; @liu_does_2025; @zhu_meritocratic_2025].

Despite its contributions, this study has limitations that point to future research. The scale is deliberately minimalist, with only two items per factor, which limits content coverage and reliability. The design also includes only two waves and two school levels, restricting the analysis of longer developmental trajectories. In addition, cross-level non-invariance suggests the need to refine perceived-meritocracy items and test for differential item functioning. Future research should expand the item pool, include more waves and age groups, better distinguish types of non-meritocratic beliefs, and examine how these dimensions relate to inequality legitimation across school contexts. Overall, this study provides a stronger foundation for analyzing how school-based meritocratic socialization shapes interpretations of fairness and inequality.
